items.title: CMS appeals Clover MA stars lawsuit - Healthcare Dive
items.reason: This item reports CMS litigation involving a Medicare Advantage payer (Clover Health), which falls directly within the scope of payer litigation and CMS/Medicare Advantage enforcement actions.
items.summary: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) has filed an appeal in a lawsuit involving Clover Health, a Medicare Advantage insurer. The lawsuit concerns alleged violations related to CMS Star Ratings, which are quality metrics used to assess Medicare Advantage plan performance. This litigation represents a regulatory enforcement action by CMS against a payer and is relevant to revenue-cycle operations as it may influence how Medicare Advantage plans manage networks, claims processing, and provider relationships going forward.

items.title: Mike Mazzei attacks Oklahoma Attorney General lawsuits against insurers - Beinsure
items.reason: The item reports on Oklahoma Attorney General lawsuits against insurers, which falls directly under payer litigation and regulatory enforcement actions that affect healthcare revenue cycle operations.
items.summary: Oklahoma state legislator Mike Mazzei has criticized the state's Attorney General for pursuing lawsuits against health insurers. The article references enforcement actions by Oklahoma's Attorney General targeting insurers, representing regulatory litigation that can impact payer operations, claims practices, and revenue-cycle compliance obligations. Such state-level enforcement actions against payers are directly relevant to healthcare systems managing claims adjudication and payer relationships.

items.title: New CMS data spotlight continued rise in No Surprises Act disputes
items.reason: CMS data on No Surprises Act arbitration disputes directly addresses payer enforcement outcomes and regulatory trends affecting claims-denial and billing practices, which are core revenue-cycle concerns.
items.summary: CMS data reveals a continuing rise in out-of-network billing disputes reaching arbitration under the No Surprises Act, a trend that health plans view negatively. The No Surprises Act protects consumers from unexpected out-of-network bills by capping patient liability and establishing an independent dispute resolution process when payers and providers disagree on payment rates. The growth in arbitration cases indicates increasing friction between payers and out-of-network providers over claim denials and payment determinations. This trend directly impacts revenue-cycle teams by expanding the volume of cases requiring arbitration involvement and potentially affecting cash flow timing and collection strategies for out-of-network claims.

items.title: Judge throws out UnitedHealth’s defamation lawsuit against the Guardian - Star Tribune
items.reason: This is litigation involving a major payer (UnitedHealth/UnitedHealthcare) and a judicial ruling on a lawsuit, which directly fits the payer litigation category, though the defamation claim itself is not directly healthcare-revenue-cycle-operations related.
items.summary: A judge dismissed UnitedHealth's defamation lawsuit against the Guardian, a news organization. While the excerpt does not provide specific details about the ruling rationale, effective date, or damages sought, this represents a significant litigation outcome for one of the nation's largest health insurers. The dismissal may have implications for how payers manage public relations and their ability to pursue legal action against critical media coverage. Revenue-cycle teams should monitor the full ruling to assess any broader implications for payer enforcement strategies or claims practices.
